narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' profiles to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall background, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles, LSAT score, family income bracket, and planned full-time enrollment, and it calculated a 68% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which is well below the average of 5.53 and pushed the prediction firmly toward expecting bar failure by signaling weaker performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 18, far under the dataset average of 36.70, was the second-biggest factor, further increasing the model's concern about your bar exam readiness. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success since it aligns closely with what successful students typically show. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile stands out for these lower academic deciles and LSAT, which often correlate with first-try struggles in the data, despite your average GPA and middle-income family background that matches many others. While full-time status is common (over 92% of students), it didn't sway things much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many from similar backgrounds succeed with stronger early signals—but the model saw clear risks based on these key academic markers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later grade deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying with updated credentials that better match bar-passing profiles. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements in these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically next time.
